subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1200 and 1450, states across the globe maintained power through combinations of religious ideology, administrative innovation, and agricultural intensification, while Buddhism, Islam, and Christianity each spread along trade and missionary networks to shape diverse regional societies.

core_concepts.title: Song Dynasty & Civil Service
core_concepts.detail: The Song Dynasty maintained power through Neo-Confucian ideology and a civil service examination system that created a merit-based bureaucracy loyal to the emperor rather than hereditary nobles, while agricultural innovations like Champa rice and the Grand Canal supported population growth and commercial expansion.

core_concepts.title: Dar al-Islam & Islamic Scholarship
core_concepts.detail: Dar al-Islam stretched from Spain to Indonesia not as a single empire but as a shared religious and commercial framework; Sufi missionaries spread Islam through spiritual accommodation of local practices, while the House of Wisdom in Baghdad synthesized Greek, Persian, and Indian knowledge into original scholarship.

core_concepts.title: European Feudalism & the Church
core_concepts.detail: European feudalism organized political authority through hereditary land grants and personal loyalty bonds rather than merit or law, creating a decentralized system where the Catholic Church wielded enormous political, economic, and spiritual power alongside secular lords.

core_concepts.title: Mesoamerican Agricultural Innovation
core_concepts.detail: Mesoamerican and Andean civilizations developed sophisticated agricultural solutions to challenging environments — chinampas on Lake Texcoco, waru-waru raised fields in Andean wetlands, and terraced hillside farming — supporting large urban centers like Cahokia and Tenochtitlan.

core_concepts.title: Coercive Labor Systems
core_concepts.detail: States across Afro-Eurasia and the Americas developed coercive labor systems (serfdom, mita, corvée) because pre-market economies required political authority rather than wages to mobilize large labor forces for agriculture and state infrastructure.

core_concepts.title: The Great Schism & the Crusades
core_concepts.detail: The Great Schism (1054) permanently divided Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox branches, weakening Christian unity against external threats; the Crusades (1095–1291) failed militarily but intensified Christian-Islamic commercial and cultural contact.

core_concepts.title: Syncretism & State Legitimacy
core_concepts.detail: Syncretism — the blending of multiple religious traditions — was a common feature of state-building across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and sub-Saharan Africa, where rulers combined Hindu, Buddhist, and indigenous traditions to legitimize authority over diverse populations.

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests comparison across world regions in 1200–1450 — practice comparing state-building methods (Song civil service vs. European feudalism vs. Inca mit'a), the spread of belief systems (Islam, Buddhism, Christianity), and how rulers used religion, architecture, and ideology to legitimize power. The 'comparison' and 'contextualization' reasoning processes dominate Unit 1 questions.
